ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers cooked onion broadly; no sauteed-specific sub-code exists. Used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for sauteed onion without risk of fabrication; left null.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sauteed onion as a side dish or topping is approximately 80 g, equivalent to roughly half a medium onion cooked down in oil.

nutrient_source.title: Onion, cooked, sauteed — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance with confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values preserved as reported. HPB Singapore Foodo is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food composition data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for sauteed onion in the University of Sydney GI Database. Raw onion GI is reported at approximately 10–15 (low). Sauteed onion concentrates natural sugars slightly through caramelisation; GI estimated at 15 (low) based on the closest analogous raw onion entry from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, AJCN). Glycemic load calculated as: GI (15) × available carbohydrate per serving (7.86 g × 0.80 serving fraction = 6.29 g) / 100 = 0.94, rounded to 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium at 12 mg/100 g; quercetin content may support vascular health
hypertension.guidance: Sauteed onion is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its very low sodium content (12 mg/100 g); however, patients should avoid adding salt during cooking and use minimal oil to prevent excess caloric intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15) and low glycemic load per serving (~0.9); moderate carbohydrate at 7.86 g/100 g with 1.7 g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sauteed onion has a low glycemic index and low glycemic load, making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control is advised as caramelisation concentrates natural sugars, and patients should avoid adding sugar or sweet glazes during preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.2 g/100 g), no trans fat, and quercetin polyphenol content with potential anti-platelet and anti-inflammatory effects
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sauteed onion is appropriate for individuals with cardiovascular disease given its low saturated fat content and absence of trans fat; using unsaturated oils (e.g., olive oil) for sauteing maximises the cardiovascular benefit of quercetin and minimises saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Quercetin polyphenol retained after cooking; low saturated fat; however, no omega-3, cruciferous, or fermented food benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sauteed onion is not contraindicated in rheumatoid arthritis and provides quercetin with modest anti-inflammatory potential, but it does not meet the threshold of a positive anti-inflammatory food; patients should use extra-virgin olive oil and avoid overcooking to preserve quercetin content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sauteed onion (also spelled sautéed onion) is a preparation of sliced or diced onions cooked in oil or butter over medium heat until softened, translucent, and lightly caramelised. The cooking process breaks down the onion's cell structure, releasing natural sugars and producing a sweet, mellow flavour with a soft, tender texture that contrasts with the sharp pungency of raw onion. Caramelisation concentrates the natural sugars slightly, raising the estimated sugar content to approximately 5.5 g/100 g. Per 100 g, sauteed onion provides approximately 123 kcal, 0.95 g protein, 10.8 g fat (predominantly from the cooking oil), 7.86 g carbohydrate, 1.7 g dietary fibre, and only 12 mg sodium, making it a low-sodium, low-glycemic-index food. The fat content reflects the oil used during cooking rather than the onion itself. Sauteed onion is a globally ubiquitous culinary preparation used as a base, topping, or side dish across Western, Asian, Middle Eastern, and Latin American cuisines. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing hypertension (very low sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (low GI ~15, low GL ~0.9), and cardiovascular disease (low saturated fat, no trans fat). Quercetin, a polyphenol retained after cooking, provides modest anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The food is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and dairy-free.
common_user_queries: How many calories in sauteed onion?
Is sauteed onion good for diabetics?
Is caramelized onion high in sugar?
Can I eat sauteed onion with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. HPB is an approved primary regional source. GI is estimated from the closest analogous raw onion entry in the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source; the estimation basis is clearly documented. Saturated fat, sugar, potassium, and cholesterol values are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked onion (NDB 11284) as a secondary cross-reference, consistent with the HPB primary values.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for sauteed onion is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry; these values depend on the specific oil used for sauteing and are not determinable from the HPB record alone. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific HPB sauteed onion entry is not reported in the available HPB Foodo data. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond calcium and iron is not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this food item. 4. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0: trans fat is absent in this preparation assuming plant-based cooking oil; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central cooked onion reference. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is set to 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived components. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is set to 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based food not fortified with vitamin D. 7. cholesterol_mg is set to 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based food. 8. sugar_g (5.5 g/100 g) and potassium_mg (130 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for onions cooked (NDB 11284) as a cross-reference supplement to the HPB record, which does not separately report these fields. 9. saturated_fat_g (1.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked onion with oil, consistent with the total fat of 10.8 g/100 g reported by HPB. 10. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on raw onion GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct sauteed onion GI entry exists in the approved GI database. 11. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 A00GJ are approximate matches; no exact sauteed onion code exists in either system without risk of fabrication.
